title: Pre recorded Lecture Slides Report Writing
body:
Social Entrepreneurship Practicum

Lecture:     Report Writing


 Dr Linh Nguyen, Course Convenor
 Lecturer
 UNSW Centre for Social Impact
 Email: linh-nguyen@unsw.edu.au
  Acknowledgement of Country

 Different Types of Reports
  Academic Research Report


  Business Consulting Report


  Financial Report


  Case Study Report


  Feasibility Report


  Risk Management Report


  Etc
 Business Consulting Report
 A consulting report
▪   A consulting report is a document that
    delivers recommendations and insights
    to a business or organization, derived
    from research and analysis conducted
    by a consultant.

▪   The format of a consulting report varies
    significantly based on the consultant's
    expertise and the specific issues the
    client is facing.
 Key Questions to get started?
      What is the primary objective of this report, and what specific
      needs or requirements is it intended to address?


      What key insights, conclusions, or actionable information
      should the reader/audience gain from this report?


      How do you expect the intended audience to use the report's
      findings, data, and analysis?
 Purpose of the Business Consulting Report
The purpose of the Business Consulting Report (Client Report) is
outlined in the project brief:

 • Project description
 • Project deliverables

The information provided in these sections indicate the purpose
of the project and the deliverables that the team must aim to
deliver.
 Report Structure
1.   Cover page
2.   Acknowledgments
3.   Executive summary
4.   Table of contents
5.   Introduction
6.   Research methodology and data collection
   •    Methodology
   •    Data Sources
6. Project deliverables
       a. Findings and Analysis
       b. Recommendations
7. Brief social impact assessment plan (optional)
8. Considerations, risks or challenges
9. Conclusion
10. A reference list
11. Appendixes (Optional)
  Cover page
▪ Your cover page
  should list your client,
  team name/number,
  workshop code, the
  project name, your
  Project Manager
  (tutor)’s name and the
  names of each team
  member.
▪ Nice visualization
 Acknowledgments


     This section acknowledges any individuals or organizations
     that contributed to the completion of the report.


     It can be placed either at the beginning of the report (before
     the Executive Summary) or at the end (after the Conclusion).
 Executive summary
   Outline project’s key findings and recommendations concisely.


   Provide a high-level summary that allows the reader to quickly grasp the essence of the
   report.

   The executive summary should be before the report begins (or before the Table of
   Contents).


   Importantly, it is not an essay introduction.


   An executive summary should be approximately 200 words.
 Executive summary                                                         – Example 1
The purpose of this report is to assist Revolve Recycling (Revolve) to develop stronger          Subject matter
marketing, promotion and communication strategies that will effectively attract the university
student demographic.

Through the comprehensive use of primary and secondary data, this report has made                Methodology/methods of analysis
significant findings and provided valuable recommendations to strengthen Revolve’s
approach.

Our findings on the student demographic include international students as a dominant             Findings
subgroup of university students interested in bikes, affordability as a barrier impacting
purchasing decisions, bicycle and road safety as a concern, minimal access to reliable
storage for bicycles as a limitation use and sustainability as a significant purchasing
consideration. Our findings regarding Revolve’s marketing position were analysed against the
Push, Pull and Share marketing strategy which found that although Revolve successfully
engages in push and pull promotional endeavours, there is great opportunity available for
expansion using a Share strategy.

Our recommendations for Revolve are to leverage social media engagement and platforms to
expand their reach, introduce student discount programs to encourage purchase decisions,         Recommendations
collaborate or partner with university and cycling societies to build a positive perception
within the student demographic, introduce merchandise to strengthen brand identity and host
pop-up and community workshop events to gain meaningful exposure.



Source: LARA, LIHARA, ALEXA, RITIKA, HAYLEY, MALIKA (Group 2 – COMM3030-T3-2024)
 Executive summary                                         – Example 2
This report provides an analysis and evaluation of the current and prospective        Subject matter
profitability, liquidity and financial stability of Outdoor Equipment Ltd.

Methods of analysis include trend, horizontal and vertical analyses as well as        Methodology/methods of
ratios such as Debt, Current and Quick ratios. Other calculations include rates of    analysis
return on Shareholders Equity and Total Assets and earnings per share to name a
few.

All calculations can be found in the appendices. Results of data analysed show        Findings
that all ratios are below industry averages. In particular, comparative performance
is poor in the areas of profit margins, liquidity, credit control, and inventory
management. The report finds the prospects of the company in its current
position are not positive. The major areas of weakness require further
investigation and remedial action by management.

Recommendations discussed include:                                                    Recommendations

•    improving/increasing inventory turnover·
•    reducing prepayments and perhaps increasing inventory levels
•    improving the average collection period for accounts receivable


Source: https://unilearning.uow.edu.au/report/4bi1.html
 Table of contents:   “paint a picture” of what’s to come
 Introduction
The introduction can be
concise but informative. This
section should include:
▪ A background information
  about the client organization
  and the project.
▪ An outline the report
  structure
 Research methodology and data collection
Methodology: Briefly describe the
research methods employed (for
example, literature review,
observations, etc.)
Data Sources
▪ Secondary Data (essential):
▪ Primary Data (optional)
▪ Data Limitations: Acknowledge
  any potential limitations
  associated with the chosen
  methods and data
 Project deliverables
This is the core of your report. The layout or structure     •   Project deliverables are useful, practical,
chosen for this section may vary and can be adaptable or
tailored to different project needs. However, this section       actionable and innovative.
should include the below elements.
                                                             •   Recommended solutions are insightful, well
      a. Findings and Analysis
                                                                 thought through, and clearly matched to the
•   Detailed analysis of data and research                       opportunity with excellent supporting analysis
    findings (consider organizing the findings
    according to the specific themes or project                  and evidence.
    deliverables outlined in the brief )
                                                             •   Project deliverables are highly aligned to what
•   Interpretation of results, discussion of key                 has been requested in the client brief.
    insights and their implications for the client
    organization                                             •   Put you in the entrepreneurs’ position. What
      b. Recommendations                                         insights and recommendations do you value?
•   Offers actionable recommendations based                      (Try to avoid general or impractical
    on the analysis and findings.
                                                                 suggestions)
•   Outlines steps for implementation.
 Structure and Format
   Use clear headings and subheadings for each section and subsection.


   They are the reader’s first impression of what you are going to present


   Ensure headings reflect the relationship between different parts of the report.


   Choose headings carefully as they appear in both the Table of Contents and report body.


   Maintain consistency in heading style throughout the document.
 Appendixes (Optional)
▪ The appendix can be used to include additional information that you think
  can help the marker/ client/ reader better understand the context of your
  findings.
▪ However, if the tables, graphs, diagrams, etc are crucial and directly
  related to your analysis, please include them within the main body of your
  report).
 Tables, Figures, Charts
▪ Refer each figure or table in
  the text.
▪ Explain how the figure or
  table connect to your ideas.
▪ Refer to items by title and
  number, especially when a
  series of items included.
 Audience and Writing Style
▪   Audience: Business/industry (entrepreneurs, business
    professionals, industry experts)
▪   Business Writing Style
      ▪ Clarity and conciseness
      ▪ Action-oriented and solution-focused approach: Clearly
         outline actionable strategies and their potential impact.
         Focus on practical implications and recommendations.
      ▪ Data-driven insights: Support arguments with relevant
         data and market analysis.
      ▪ Visual aids: Incorporate relevant charts, graphs, and
         tables to support key points.
 Group Report Writing & Teamwork
 Group Report Writing & Teamwork
Not recommended                                                      Recommended ☺
Approach 1: Single Author Approach:                                   Approach 3 – Collaborative Writing Process
▪    One team member writes the entire report.                        ▪    Assign a lead writer and a reviewer for each section or task.
                                                                           Ensure every team member is involved in the process.
▪    This often results in a limited range of ideas and prevents
     other team members from learning through the report              ▪    Before finalization, have all team members review the entire
     preparation process.                                                  document.
Approach 2: Divided Sections Approach:                                ▪    Share responsibility for proofreading and editing among all
                                                                           team members.
▪    Each team member writes a separate section. Then the
     group put individual parts to complete the report.
▪    There’s no discussion to connect individual parts to formulate
     a coherent report.
▪    This typically leads to a disjointed report lacking coherence.
▪    Team members also miss out on learning about topics
     outside their assigned section.
